summary: IDAllocator correctly implements a mutex-serialized, sidecar-file-persisted monotonic fileID counter. All required tests pass under -race, go vet and go build are clean. No correctness bug found in the allocator itself. Two architecturally significant risks are flagged for follow-up: (1) sidecar-vs-catalog.dat coupling has no cross-validation, so a missing/lost/restored-independently .idalloc file next to a non-fresh catalog.dat would silently reset fileID allocation to 1 and collide with existing records; this is out of scope for this isolated-allocator subtask but MUST be addressed when catalog CRUD wiring lands. (2) the concurrent test takes ~45s due to 10,000 synchronous fsyncs, which is a CI cost worth a maintainability note.

requirements.evidence: Next() starts at 1, strictly increasing, never reuses IDs even after conceptual deletes (no delete path exists yet in this subtask, but design comment correctly states the intent). InvalidFileID=0 reserved as sentinel. High-water-mark persisted via WriteAt+Sync to sidecar file idAllocSuffix='.idalloc', restored on NewIDAllocator. Matches docs/LLD/catalog.md's 'monotonically increasing atomic counter, no reuse, no gaps-matter semantics'.

architecture_conformance.evidence: Sidecar file path is derived via fm.file.Name() (idalloc.go:78), which is the exact same package (catalog) accessing an unexported field on FileManager -- not reflection or cross-package reach-in, so it's not fragile to normal refactors (a rename of `file` would be a compile error, caught immediately). However, os.File.Name() returns exactly the string passed to OpenFile (not a resolved absolute path), so if a future caller opens the same catalog via two different relative/absolute path spellings, the sidecar file's identity diverges from the catalog file's identity -- this is a latent multi-instance-path risk, not exercised by tests, but plausible in production if callers aren't disciplined about path normalization. The design comment block (lines 16-39) thoroughly documents why a sidecar file was chosen over reusing file.go's page-0 bitmap or a fixed data page -- good architectural rationale, consistent with keeping 1.1.3's on-disk format untouched.

regression_risk.evidence: CORE RISK (prompt point 2/3): the sidecar (.idalloc) and catalog.dat (.meta/catalog.dat) are two independently-openable files with no shared transaction/commit protocol. Next() itself is safe in isolation -- it durably syncs the new high-water-mark BEFORE returning the ID to the caller, so no code path within IDAllocator can hand out ID N without N already being durable in the sidecar. The genuine architectural risk is external: (a) NewIDAllocator creates the sidecar file with O_CREATE if missing (idalloc.go:80) and silently treats a missing/empty sidecar as 'next=0' (a fresh catalog) with no check against catalog.dat's actual contents. If a non-fresh catalog.dat ever has its sidecar lost independently (deleted, not copied along in a backup/restore, wiped by an incomplete migration, etc.), IDAllocator will silently re-hand-out IDs 1, 2, 3... that collide with FileIDs already recorded in existing catalog records. There is no defensive check (e.g. cross-referencing the catalog's own highest-known FileID) to detect or reject this. (b) There is no atomicity/ordering enforcement between 'catalog record for fileID N becomes durable' and 'sidecar records high-water-mark >= N' beyond the natural fact that callers must call Next() first to obtain N. This holds only as long as every future write path obtains fileIDs exclusively via IDAllocator.Next() and the sidecar file is always co-located/backed-up atomically with catalog.dat -- neither of which is enforced by any code in this diff. This is explicitly out of scope for 1.1.4 (which tests the allocator in isolation) but MUST be flagged and addressed in the catalog CRUD wiring subtask, e.g. by validating the sidecar's high-water-mark against the max FileID actually present in catalog.dat on open, or storing the high-water-mark in the same durability domain as the catalog file itself.

edge_cases.evidence: Fresh sidecar (size 0) correctly seeds next=0 so first Next() = 1. Any size other than 0 or 8 bytes is rejected with a clear error (idalloc.go:103-106), guarding against partial-write corruption being silently accepted. Close() closes the sidecar handle.

security.evidence: No injection/traversal concerns; path is derived from the already-validated FileManager path. File permissions 0o644 consistent with file.go's catalog file permissions.

performance.evidence: Every Next() call does a synchronous WriteAt+Sync (fsync), which is correct for durability but means throughput is bound by disk fsync latency; the concurrent test (10,000 calls) takes ~45s in this environment. This is inherent to the requirement (durable high-water-mark on every allocation) rather than an implementation flaw, but worth flagging as a maintainability/CI-cost concern (see maintainability).

maintainability.evidence: The concurrent subtest's ~45s runtime (10,000 synchronous fsyncs) is a real but modest CI cost. Recommend considering a `-short` mode with reduced iteration count (e.g. 20x20=400) for fast local/CI runs, keeping the full 100x100 as an opt-in/nightly test, so `go test ./...` doesn't accumulate this cost across many packages over time. Not a blocking issue for this subtask.

confidence.evidence: Code was read in full, tests were actually executed (not just inspected) with -race, go vet and go build were run and are clean. The one genuine architectural gap (cross-file durability coupling) is understood and traced to its root cause, and is explicitly acknowledged as out-of-scope for this subtask per its own test spec (which only tests the allocator paired with its own freshly-created sidecar).

test_coverage.evidence: TestFileIDAllocator has all three required subtests: sequential strict monotonic increase from 1 (asserts id==prev+1 and first==1, not just uniqueness), concurrent 100x100=10,000 unique IDs (exact counts asserted via len(seen)==total and count==total), and persist+reopen restoring correct high-water-mark with strict maxID+1 continuation. All ran and passed under -race. Full package suite (TestCatalogFileManager, TestSlottedPage*, TestRecordEncodeDecode, etc.) also passes, no regressions.

possible_improvements.evidence: 1) Add a cross-check on NewIDAllocator (or at the future catalog-CRUD wiring layer) that the sidecar's high-water-mark is >= the max FileID actually present in catalog.dat, rejecting/repairing otherwise, to close the two-file coupling gap. 2) Consider deriving the sidecar path more robustly (e.g. via filepath.Clean/Abs) to avoid path-spelling divergence. 3) Add a `-short`-aware reduced-iteration variant of the concurrent test for fast CI runs.

results.TestFileIDAllocator: PASS (45.68s, dominated by concurrent subtest fsyncs)
